request.question: Which plant grows further North, Zamia or Vallea?

title: Salvia divinorum
Mendoncia velloziana
Gaultheria nummularioides
Zamia
Carex stricta
Sisymbrium altissimum
Vallea
Argemone albiflora
Chorizanthe watsonii
Delphinium staphisagria
sentences: Salvia divinorum (also known as sage of the diviners, ska maría pastora, seer's sage, yerba de la pastora and just salvia) is a psychoactive plant which can induce visions and other spiritual experiences. Its native habitat is in cloud forest in the isolated Sierra Mazateca of Oaxaca, Mexico, where it grows in shady and moist locations.
The plant grows to over a meter high, has hollow square stems, large leaves, and occasional white flowers with violet calyxes.
Botanists have not determined whether "Salvia divinorum" is a cultigen or a hybrid; native plants reproduce vegetatively, rarely producing viable seed.
Mendoncia velloziana is a plant native to Atlantic Coast restingas vegetation which is an ecosystem of Atlantic Forest biome.
In addition, this plant grows either in Cerrado vegetation of Brazil.
This plant grows in following states of Brazil: Bahia, Ceará Minas Gerais Rio de Janeiro, São Paulo, Paraná and Santa Catarina, and it is usually visited by the hummingbirds.
"Gaultheria nummularioides" is a species of plant in the family Ericaceae.
It is distributed across India, Myanmar, Nepal, Bhutan, Indonesia, and Southwest China (Tibet, Yunnan, and Sichuan).
The plant grows in areas between 1,700 and 3,000 m above sea level, and grows better on rocky mountainsides and weed tree forests.
This species has been cultivated as an ornamental in Britain, the United States, and elsewhere for some decades.
Zamia is a genus of cycad of the family Zamiaceae, native to Mexico, the West Indies, and Central and South America as far south as Bolivia.
The range of one species ("Z. integrifolia", extends into the contiguous United States, i.e. Georgia and Florida.
Carex stricta is a species of sedge known by the common names upright sedge and tussock sedge.
It is grass-like and can be difficult to distinguish from other plants, because of its long, triangular, green stems.
The plant grows in moist marshes, forests and alongside bodies of water.
It grows up to 2 ft tall and 2 ft wide.
When the leaves die, they build on top of or around the living plant, making a "tussock".
It is one of the most common wetland sedges in eastern North America.
Sisymbrium altissimum is a species of "Sisymbrium".
The plant is native to the western part of the Mediterranean Basin in Europe and Northern Africa and is widely naturalized throughout most of the world, including all of North America.
It was probably introduced into North America by a contaminant crop seed.
The plant grows in soils of all textures, even sand.
The plant germinates in winter or early spring.
The blooming time is lengthy, and after maturity the plant forms a tumbleweed.

Common names of the plant include Jim Hill mustard, after James J. Hill, a Canadian-American railroad magnate, tall tumblemustard, tall mustard, tumble mustard, tumbleweed mustard, tall sisymbrium, and tall hedge mustard.
Vallea is a genus of trees in the family Elaeocarpaceae.
Trees in this genus are native to the Andes mountain range in South America and are classified in two species:
Argemone albiflora, the white prickly poppy, also known as the bluestem prickly poppy or the Texas prickly poppy, is a small erect plant with a decorative white flower with a yellow latex.
It is deeply rooted with yellow or red stamens.
The plant is known for the sharp prickles on its stem and leaves.
The sepals fall off as the flower of this plant grows bigger.
It grows in the arid regions of the southern Midwest along roadsides and disturbed pieces of land.
Native Americans have long revered this plant for its medicinal and other uses.
Chorizanthe watsonii is a species of flowering plant in the buckwheat family known by the common name fivetooth spineflower.
It is native to the western United States from Washington to the Mojave Desert.
It grows in many types of plant communities from desert scrub to woodland and sagebrush.
This small plant grows a woolly erect stem up to about 15 centimeters tall.
The inflorescence is a cluster of flowers surrounded by five hairy greenish bracts tipped with hooked awns.
The flower is about 2 millimeters wide and yellow in color.
Delphinium staphisagria is a species of "Delphinium", or larkspur, of the family Ranunculaceae.
This plant is also known as Lice-Bane or Stavesacre.
It is described botanically as a stoutly-stemmed, hairy biennial with large palmate leaves up to 6 inches (15 cm) across.
The flowers are mauve-blue to blue, short-spurred, and up to 1 inch (2.5 cm) across, occurring in racemes.
The plant grows to a height of 4–5 feet.
It grows throughout the Mediterranean.

The dark-colored, wrinkled seeds of "D. staphisagria" are characteristically quite large (~5x6 mm), and it is likely that the species name, which translates to "wild raisin" (classical Greek: σταφὶς ἀγρία) is based on their appearance.
This name-derivation seems to have been arrived at independently by a modern horticulturalist, David Bassett, who also gives a detailed account of his experiences in growing this species.
All parts of this plant are highly toxic and should not be ingested in any quantity.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Which plant grows further North, Zamia or Vallea?
hop1_docs: Zamia variegata | Zamia variegata is a species of plant in the family Zamiaceae.  It is native to Belize, Guatemala, Honduras, and Mexico.  It grows in forests.  It is threatened by habitat destruction, which has likely reduced the population by about 50% over the last few decades.

Zamia dressleri | Zamia dressleri is a species of plant in the family Zamiaceae family.  It is endemic to Panama, where it grows in rainforest habitat.  It is found in Colón and Comarca de San Blas provinces.

Zamia purpurea | Zamia purpurea is a species of plant in the family Zamiaceae.  It is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs only in the states of Oaxaca and Veracruz.  It grows in the understory of rainforests.  It is affected by habitat destruction.
Zamia acuminata | Zamia acuminata is a species of plant in the Zamiaceae family which is threatened by habitat loss.  It is found in Costa Rica, Nicaragua, and Panama.

Zamia encephalartoides | Zamia encephalartoides is a species of plant in the Zamiaceae family.  It is endemic to Colombia.  It is threatened by habitat loss.  It is found in only two locations in Santander Province, Colombia, which are near the Chicamocha River and Umpala River.

Zamia gentryi | Zamia gentryi is a species of plant in the Zamiaceae family.  It is endemic to Ecuador.  It is threatened by habitat loss.  It is found in two locations of Carchi Province and Esmeraldas Province, Ecuador, which are located near Alto Tambo, as well as between Lita and San Lorenzo.

Macrozamia riedlei | Macrozamia riedlei, commonly known as zamia or zamia palm, is a species of plant in the Zamiaceae family.  The Noongar names for the plant are baian, djiriji, koondagoor and quinning.
response.summary_1: Evidence: several Zamia species occur as far north as Mexico — e.g., "Zamia variegata ... native to Belize, Guatemala, Honduras, and Mexico" and "Zamia purpurea ... endemic to Mexico." Missing fact / bridge: the northernmost range (country or latitude) of Vallea (genus or its species) is not present. Retrieval direction: fetch Vallea distribution (search for "Vallea distribution", "Vallea range", or specific species such as "Vallea stipularis range") to determine whether any Vallea occurs north of Mexico or at a more northern latitude than the Zamia records above.
response.hop2_query: Vallea northernmost range distribution Vallea stipularis range
hop2_docs: Vallea stipularis | Vallea stipularis is a species of tree in the Elaeocarpaceae family.  It is native from the Andes mountains in South America.
Vallea | Vallea is a genus of trees in the family Elaeocarpaceae.  Trees in this genus are native to the Andes mountain range in South America and are classified in two species:

Vallea ecuadorensis | Vallea ecuadorensis is a species of tree in the Elaeocarpaceae family.  Although formerly considered endemic to Ecuador it has also been collected in Bolivia and Peru.  This species occurs in subtropical or tropical moist montane forests, at 2500 – 3500 meters above sea level.

Sentinel Range (Canada) | The Sentinel Range is one of the northernmost sub-ranges of the Canadian Rockies, lying between Muncho Lake (SW) and the Liard River (N).  The northernmost is the Terminal Range, so named for its position at the terminus of the Rockies, and lies to its west.

Metrosideros stipularis | Metrosideros stipularis is a species of the myrtle family commonly known as tepú, trepú, or tepual.  It is an evergreen tree or shrub that can attain a height of about seven metres.  The plant is native to southern South America in the southern portions of Chile and Argentina and is a typical resident of very wet areas, especially peat bogs. Tepú has white flowers that emerge during the austral summer from January through March.  The tree's wood is used within its range as a firewood due to it high energy content.

Stokes Range | The Stokes Range () is a mountain range on Bathurst Island, Nunavut, Canada.  The range is one of the northernmost ranges in the world and of the Arctic Cordillera.  Its highest point is 412 m at Stokes Mountain.

Grinnell Range | The Grinnell Range is a mountain range on northwestern Devon Island, Nunavut, Canada.  It is one of the northernmost mountain ranges in the world forming part of the Arctic Cordillera mountain system.
response.summary_2: Zamia reaches as far north as Mexico, while Vallea is native to the Andes in South America (e.g., V. stipularis and V. ecuadorensis occur in Ecuador/Bolivia/Peru), so Zamia grows further north than Vallea.
